cases.title: Salicylate toxicity (mixed respiratory alkalosis + HAGMA)
cases.clinical_stem: 27-year-old presents with nausea, hyperventilation, and tinnitus after ingesting a large quantity of tablets.

questions_flow.label: Primary acid-base disorder
questions_flow.prompt: What is the primary acid-base disorder?

questions_flow.label: Anion gap
questions_flow.prompt: What is the anion gap status?

expected_compensation.note: Low PaCO2 and low HCO3 are due to two primary processes: respiratory alkalosis and HAGMA

cases.learning_objective: Recognise the mixed respiratory alkalosis and high anion gap metabolic acidosis of salicylate toxicity
cases.explanation: Both PaCO2 and HCO3 are low. This is not explained by a single primary disorder alone. The low PaCO2 indicates a respiratory alkalosis, while the low HCO3 with raised anion gap (23.0) indicates a high anion gap metabolic acidosis. This is a mixed respiratory alkalosis and metabolic acidosis, classic for salicylate toxicity.

cases.title: Simple respiratory alkalosis
cases.clinical_stem: 24-year-old presents with fast breathing, hand tingling, and escalating distress without focal findings.

questions_flow.label: Primary acid-base disorder
questions_flow.prompt: What is the primary acid-base disorder?

cases.learning_objective: Recognise acute respiratory alkalosis with appropriate metabolic compensation
cases.explanation: High pH indicates alkalaemia. Low PaCO2 indicates a primary respiratory alkalosis. For acute respiratory alkalosis, expected HCO3 is ~21.2; measured 21.6 is appropriate acute compensation. The anion gap is normal at 12.0. This pattern is consistent with simple hyperventilation.

cases.title: COPD exacerbation (acute-on-chronic respiratory acidosis)
cases.clinical_stem: 68-year-old presents with productive cough with increased work of breathing, hypercapnic symptoms, and known COPD.

questions_flow.label: Primary acid-base disorder
questions_flow.prompt: What is the primary acid-base disorder?

cases.explanation: COPD exacerbations cause acute rises in CO2 on a background of chronic respiratory acidosis.

cases.title: Lactic acidosis (sepsis)
cases.clinical_stem: 72-year-old is brought in with tachycardic, with confusion, with rigors, and with suspected sepsis.

questions_flow.label: Primary acid-base disorder
questions_flow.prompt: What is the primary acid-base disorder?

questions_flow.label: Anion gap
questions_flow.prompt: What is the anion gap status?

cases.explanation: Sepsis commonly causes high anion gap metabolic acidosis due to lactate accumulation.

cases.title: Simple respiratory acidosis
cases.clinical_stem: 37-year-old presents with reduced consciousness and hypoventilation without focal findings.

questions_flow.label: Primary acid-base disorder
questions_flow.prompt: What is the primary acid-base disorder?

cases.learning_objective: Recognise acute respiratory acidosis with appropriate metabolic compensation
cases.explanation: Low pH indicates acidaemia. High PaCO2 indicates a primary respiratory acidosis. For acute respiratory acidosis, expected HCO3 is ~26.3; measured 25.7 is appropriate acute compensation. The anion gap is normal at 8.0. This pattern is consistent with acute hypoventilation.

cases.title: COPD (chronic respiratory acidosis with metabolic compensation)
cases.clinical_stem: 65-year-old with chronic respiratory symptoms presents with worsening shortness of breath over several days.

questions_flow.label: Primary acid-base disorder
questions_flow.prompt: What is the primary acid-base disorder?

cases.learning_objective: Recognise chronic respiratory acidosis with appropriate renal compensation
cases.explanation: pH is low or near-normal with elevated PaCO2, indicating respiratory acidosis. In chronic respiratory acidosis, HCO3 should rise by ~4 mmol/L per 10 mmHg PaCO2 above 40. Expected HCO3 is ~30.4; measured 30.8 is appropriately elevated, consistent with chronic respiratory compensation seen in COPD.

cases.title: Vomiting (metabolic alkalosis with respiratory compensation)
cases.clinical_stem: 67-year-old presents with dizziness and volume depletion after recurrent nausea and retching.

questions_flow.label: Primary acid-base disorder
questions_flow.prompt: What is the primary acid-base disorder?

cases.learning_objective: Recognise metabolic alkalosis with appropriate respiratory compensation
cases.explanation: High pH indicates alkalaemia. Elevated HCO3 indicates a primary metabolic alkalosis. Expected compensatory PaCO2 is ~49.1; measured 48.8 is appropriate, supporting metabolic alkalosis with expected respiratory compensation, as seen with vomiting.

cases.title: Salicylate toxicity (mixed respiratory alkalosis + HAGMA)
cases.clinical_stem: 34-year-old presents confused, tachypnoeic, and febrile with ringing in the ears.

questions_flow.label: Primary acid-base disorder
questions_flow.prompt: What is the primary acid-base disorder?

questions_flow.label: Anion gap
questions_flow.prompt: What is the anion gap status?

expected_compensation.note: Low PaCO2 and low HCO3 are due to two primary processes: respiratory alkalosis and HAGMA

cases.learning_objective: Recognise the mixed respiratory alkalosis and high anion gap metabolic acidosis of salicylate toxicity
cases.explanation: Both PaCO2 and HCO3 are low. This is not explained by a single primary disorder alone. The low PaCO2 indicates a respiratory alkalosis, while the low HCO3 with raised anion gap (21.0) indicates a high anion gap metabolic acidosis. This is a mixed respiratory alkalosis and metabolic acidosis, classic for salicylate toxicity.

cases.title: Renal failure (uraemic HAGMA with appropriate respiratory compensation)
cases.clinical_stem: 59-year-old with renal failure presents with fluid overload, lethargy, and worsening oliguria after missing dialysis.

questions_flow.label: Primary acid-base disorder
questions_flow.prompt: What is the primary acid-base disorder?

questions_flow.label: Anion gap
questions_flow.prompt: What is the anion gap status?

cases.learning_objective: Recognise high anion gap metabolic acidosis from uraemia with appropriate respiratory compensation
cases.explanation: Low pH indicates acidaemia. Low HCO3 indicates a primary metabolic acidosis. Winter's formula predicts PaCO2 ~23.0 (+/-2); measured 22.4 is appropriate compensation. Anion gap is 136 - (98 + 10) = 28, which is raised. This pattern is most consistent with renal failure causing uraemic high anion gap metabolic acidosis.

questions_flow.prompt: What is the primary acid-base disorder?

cases.learning_objective: Recognise acute respiratory alkalosis with appropriate metabolic compensation
cases.explanation: High pH indicates alkalaemia. Low PaCO2 indicates a primary respiratory alkalosis. In acute respiratory alkalosis, expected HCO3 is ~20.4; measured 20.7 is appropriate, consistent with acute hyperventilation such as panic.

cases.title: DKA (HAGMA with appropriate respiratory compensation)
cases.clinical_stem: 34-year-old presents to ED with polyuria and marked thirst and nausea and abdominal discomfort.

questions_flow.label: Primary acid-base disorder

questions_flow.label: Anion gap
questions_flow.prompt: What is the anion gap status?

cases.learning_objective: Recognise high anion gap metabolic acidosis with appropriate respiratory compensation
cases.explanation: Low pH = acidaemia. Low HCO3 indicates metabolic acidosis. Winter's formula predicts PaCO2 ~30.5 (+/-2); measured 30.4 is appropriate compensation. Anion gap is 138 - (103 + 15) = 20 (raised), consistent with HAGMA such as DKA.

cases.title: Diarrhoea (normal anion gap metabolic acidosis)
cases.clinical_stem: 70-year-old from a nursing home presents with volume depletion following ongoing lower gastrointestinal fluid loss.

questions_flow.label: Primary acid-base disorder
questions_flow.prompt: What is the primary acid-base disorder?

questions_flow.label: Anion gap
questions_flow.prompt: What is the anion gap status?

cases.learning_objective: Recognise normal anion gap metabolic acidosis with appropriate respiratory compensation
cases.explanation: The pH is in the normal range, but the low HCO3 still indicates a primary metabolic acidosis with respiratory compensation. Low HCO3 indicates a primary metabolic acidosis. Winter's formula predicts PaCO2 ~33.5 (+/-2); measured 33.3 is appropriate compensation. Anion gap is 136 - (110 + 17) = 9, which is normal, consistent with NAGMA such as diarrhoea.

cases.title: Diuretic use (metabolic alkalosis with respiratory compensation)
cases.clinical_stem: 74-year-old on diuretics presents with cramps and mild volume depletion after recent escalation of therapy for ankle swelling.

questions_flow.label: Primary acid-base disorder
questions_flow.prompt: What is the primary acid-base disorder?

cases.learning_objective: Recognise chloride-responsive metabolic alkalosis from diuretic-associated volume depletion
cases.explanation: High pH indicates alkalaemia. Elevated HCO3 indicates a primary metabolic alkalosis. Expected compensatory PaCO2 is ~44.2; measured 44.1 is appropriate. Anion gap is 138 - (96 + 30) = 12, which is normal. This pattern is most consistent with diuretic-associated contraction alkalosis from diuretic use.

cases.clinical_stem: 66-year-old on haemodialysis presents with malaise and nausea after missing a recent dialysis session.

questions_flow.label: Primary acid-base disorder
questions_flow.prompt: What is the primary acid-base disorder?

questions_flow.label: Anion gap
questions_flow.prompt: What is the anion gap status?

cases.learning_objective: Recognise high anion gap metabolic acidosis from uraemia with appropriate respiratory compensation
cases.explanation: Low pH indicates acidaemia. Low HCO3 indicates a primary metabolic acidosis. Winter's formula predicts PaCO2 ~30.5 (+/-2); measured 30.2 is appropriate compensation. Anion gap is 139 - (105 + 15) = 19, which is raised. This pattern is most consistent with renal failure causing uraemic high anion gap metabolic acidosis.

questions_flow.label: Primary acid-base disorder
questions_flow.prompt: What is the primary acid-base disorder?

cases.learning_objective: Recognise acute respiratory alkalosis with appropriate metabolic compensation
cases.explanation: High pH indicates alkalaemia. Low PaCO2 indicates a primary respiratory alkalosis. For acute respiratory alkalosis, expected HCO3 is ~22.0; measured 21.5 is appropriate acute compensation. The anion gap is normal at 9.0. This pattern is consistent with simple hyperventilation.

cases.title: Diuretic use (metabolic alkalosis with respiratory compensation)
cases.clinical_stem: 74-year-old on diuretics presents with cramps and mild volume depletion after recent escalation of therapy for ankle swelling.

questions_flow.label: Primary acid-base disorder
questions_flow.prompt: What is the primary acid-base disorder?

cases.learning_objective: Recognise chloride-responsive metabolic alkalosis from diuretic-associated volume depletion
cases.explanation: The pH is near normal, but the elevated HCO3 still indicates a primary metabolic alkalosis with compensation. Elevated HCO3 indicates a primary metabolic alkalosis. Expected compensatory PaCO2 is ~44.2; measured 44.5 is appropriate. Anion gap is 137 - (96 + 30) = 11, which is normal. This pattern is most consistent with diuretic-associated contraction alkalosis from diuretic use.

cases.learning_objective: Recognise normal anion gap metabolic acidosis with appropriate respiratory compensation

Winter's formula predicts PaCO2 ~29.0 (+/-2); measured 28.5 is appropriate compensation. Anion gap is 139 - (117 + 14) = 8, which is normal, consistent with NAGMA such as diarrhoea.

cases.explanation: High pH indicates alkalaemia. Elevated HCO3 indicates a primary metabolic alkalosis. Expected compensatory PaCO2 is ~49.8; measured 47.9 is appropriate, supporting metabolic alkalosis with expected respiratory compensation, as seen with vomiting.

cases.learning_objective: Recognise acute respiratory alkalosis with appropriate metabolic compensation
cases.explanation: High pH indicates alkalaemia. Low PaCO2 indicates a primary respiratory alkalosis. In acute respiratory alkalosis, expected HCO3 is ~20.8; measured 20.7 is appropriate, consistent with acute hyperventilation such as panic.

cases.title: COPD (chronic respiratory acidosis with metabolic compensation)

questions_flow.prompt: What is the primary acid-base disorder?

cases.learning_objective: Recognise chronic respiratory acidosis with appropriate renal compensation
cases.explanation: pH is low or near-normal with elevated PaCO2, indicating respiratory acidosis. In chronic respiratory acidosis, HCO3 should rise by ~4 mmol/L per 10 mmHg PaCO2 above 40. Expected HCO3 is ~31.6; measured 32.3 is appropriately elevated, consistent with chronic respiratory compensation seen in COPD.
